title: The Lie of the Guards
subtitle: The chief priests buy silence, and the soldiers swear to a theft they slept through

stillness: Be still, and do not give yourself to sleep in this hour.
For at this hour our Redeemer rose, having broken the empire of death.
Let the rising sun not find your inner self in darkness, but awaiting the light that does not set.
gospel: Behold, some of the soldiers who kept the tomb came into the city.
They told the chief priests all that had happened.
And the priests, gathered with the elders, took counsel.
They gave a large sum of money to the soldiers, saying:
Say this — His disciples came by night and stole Him away, while we were sleeping.
And the soldiers took the money, and did as they were taught.
And this word was spread abroad among the Jews, even to this day.
behold: See the soldiers, who saw the deed and heard the angel's word, hurrying into the city on the very day of the Resurrection.
Willing or unwilling, they are now witnesses and heralds of the truth — the enemies and crucifiers of Christ hear from their own that He has risen.
See the priests gathered with the elders, plotting to forge a falsehood.

See the abundant silver, drawn not from their own purses but from the treasury of the temple and the offerings of the poor — the very kind of money with which they had earlier bought His blood and His life.
See it pressed into the soldiers' hands, to blacken the fame of the Resurrection, to pervert the truth.
See them swear to a theft they slept through — and so make sleeping men their witnesses.
consider: O foolish madness! You bring forward sleeping men as witnesses.
If they slept, how did they see the theft? And if they did not see, how are they witnesses?
And if they did see the thieves, why did they not seize them?
Truly the thing they said had the very shape of a lie.
How should poor disciples, unlettered and not daring even to appear, steal Him away?
If they fled while they still saw Christ living, how, with Him dead, would they not have feared so great a crowd of soldiers?
Could they overturn the door of the tomb? A great stone lay there, needing many hands.
Was there not also a seal set upon it?
By the very things by which they try to overshadow the Resurrection, they make it shine.
For in saying His disciples stole Him, they confess the body was not in the tomb.
The lie is shown a lie by the guard of soldiers and the terror of the disciples.
The guards confess the miracles; but those who ought to have turned to repentance after such testimony persevere in malice.
The money given for the temple's use they turn into the redemption of a lie — as before they gave thirty pieces of silver to the traitor.
O great plague of avarice, ever to be loathed by good souls! What do you leave whole, what unshaken?
The truth, told by its enemies, shines all the brighter.
But iniquity has lied to itself, and avarice — and like Christ's own betrayed disciple, they are deceived.
prayer: to blacken the fame of Your Resurrection the Jews gave a large sum of money to the soldiers, and corrupted them to pervert the truth.
Grant me that I be not corrupted by money, nor corrupt others,
nor act unjustly for things temporal and passing,
but always remain in the truth, and be set free through it.
Grant me also that, as after Your Resurrection there rose up witnesses to You,
so I too may be worthy to testify of You in word and work,
and in the general resurrection to have my portion with You and with Your elect,
and with them, before You in Your kingdom, to rejoice without end, in Your mercy. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Simplex enim aequitas, et indocta hominum rusticitas saepe veritatem rei sine fraude manifestat; at contra versuta malignitas falsitatem verisimilibus verbis pro vero commendare decertat.
latinJewel.english: Simple honesty and the unschooled plainness of men often make the truth of a thing known without deceit; but cunning malice, on the contrary, strains to commend falsehood as truth with plausible words.
